title: The Cost of Full Fine-Tuning
concept: Fine-tuning all parameters of a large model for each downstream task requires saving a complete set of weights per task, causing massive storage and deployment costs.

exercises.prompt: Why do developers want to fine-tune AI models in the first place?

options.text: To adapt a general model to a specific task.
To make the model run without electricity.
To delete all previous training data.

exercises.explain: Fine-tuning helps customize a pre-trained model for specific tasks, but doing it fully for every task becomes incredibly expensive in terms of storage and computer memory.

cards.title: The Weight of AI
cards.body: Imagine you want an AI to write poetry, write code, and translate French. If you copy and modify the entire AI for each task, you'll run out of computer storage in no time! Why is adjusting everything so expensive? Let's find out.

cards.title: What is Full Fine-Tuning?
cards.body: Full fine-tuning means taking a massive AI model and updating all of its millions or billions of mathematical weights for a new task. While it works well, it forces you to save a complete, giant copy of the model weights for every single task you train it on.

cards.title: The Closet Conundrum
cards.body: Imagine buying a brand new, giant closet for every single pair of shoes you own, rather than just changing the shoes inside one closet. That is full fine-tuning. You are duplicating the massive foundation (the entire closet) every single time you want to store a new task.

cards.title: Storage Multiplying Fast
cards.body: Suppose you have a 10-gigabyte base AI model. If you use full fine-tuning to adapt it for writing emails, writing code, and summarizing documents, you must save three separate 10-gigabyte files. That is 30 gigabytes of storage for just three simple tasks!

cards.title: The Selective Editing Myth
cards.body: People think that fine-tuning only edits the 'new' parts of an AI. In reality, full fine-tuning updates every single weight across the entire network, forcing you to save the whole giant model all over again.

cards.title: Comparing Storage Needs
cards.body: Compare how full fine-tuning scales your storage needs versus keeping a single base model.

left.text: 10GB base + 10GB task A + 10GB task B = 30GB total storage needed.

right.text: 10GB base + tiny task A file + tiny task B file = ~10.1GB total storage.

exercises.prompt: What is the main reason full fine-tuning is so expensive to deploy for multiple tasks?

options.text: It deletes the model's ability to learn new things.
It requires saving a complete, massive set of weights for each distinct task.
It only works for translation tasks.
It makes the AI run without any computer memory.

exercises.explain: Full fine-tuning updates all of the model's weights, requiring you to save and deploy a completely new, giant copy of the model for every single application.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Full fine-tuning requires you to save a complete, separate copy of the model's weights for every downstream task.
exercises.explain: Full fine-tuning modifies the entire network's parameters, meaning you cannot share the same base weights across tasks without keeping separate giant files.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe the primary drawback of full fine-tuning.
exercises.sentence: Saving a complete set of updated weights for every task leads to massive storage and deployment costs.
exercises.explain: Because full fine-tuning creates a complete copy of the model's parameters for every new task, it quickly consumes all available storage space.

exercises.prompt: Which AI assistant is demonstrating the high-cost method of full fine-tuning?

options.text: I just add a tiny adapter to the model and keep the main weights frozen.
I copy all 70 billion parameters and save a brand new 140 GB file for every single task I train on.
I write a very long prompt to guide the model without saving any weights.

exercises.explain: AI B is correct because saving a complete, independent set of parameters for every single task is the defining storage bottleneck of full fine-tuning. AI A describes parameter-efficient tuning, and AI C describes basic prompt engineering.

exercises.prompt: Why is full fine-tuning a huge problem when you want to deploy one AI model to do fifty different customer service tasks?

options.text: It makes the AI forget how to speak English entirely.
It requires saving a massive, multi-gigabyte set of weights for every single task, leading to extreme storage costs.
It deletes the original internet data the AI was trained on.
It requires the AI to run on batteries instead of plugged-in power.

exercises.explain: Keeping a full copy of a 100-billion parameter model for every single application is financially and logistically impossible for most businesses. Storage costs, download times, and server RAM requirements quickly break the bank.
